subject: [PATCH v2 0/4] iova: switch to augmented rbtree to avoid linear search

reviews.author: Robin Murphy
reviews.summary:
reviewer expressed interest in the patch series and noted that some higher-level details stand out, but did not provide specific technical concerns or suggestions

reviewer noted that the anchor node is necessary to ensure cached_node can be a valid starting point for allocation walks, and questioned its relevance if the algorithm is being completely changed

Reviewer Robin Murphy expressed concerns that the allocation algorithm may not be optimal and suggested revising it to directly walk from the root towards the highest free space under the given limit_pfn for any value of limit_pfn, potentially eliminating the need for special cases like max32_alloc_size bodge. Additionally, he is cautious about growing struct iova unnecessarily due to concerns about iova_cache usage on busy systems.

Reviewer Robin Murphy noted that the patch does not address DMA masks of 33-56 bits and suggested a general algorithm for handling such cases, which involves navigating the augmented rbtree in a specific way to find sufficient space. He also questioned whether an rbtree is still the best choice for this task.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Rik,

I'm certainly interested to see this series, as improving the allocator 
has been on my to-do list for a very long time now. It might take me a 
while to page all the details back in, but some higher-level things 
stand out already...

On 2026-05-18 7:05 pm, Rik van Riel wrote:

---

The main reason the anchor node exists is to ensure that cached_node can 
always be a valid place to start an allocation walk - if we're no longer 
using the latter, there's little point in keeping the former either, 
especially if it starts causing undesirable issues. It does also help as 
an optimisation to avoid a couple of special cases in the current 
algorithm, but again if we're completely changing the algorithm then 
those may well no longer apply either.

---

I would think that if we still need a special case for this then we 
don't really have the right allocation algorithm - I recall getting as 
far as concluding that it would be hard to do with the generic interval 
tree, but with the right subtree data I would think we ought to be able 
to walk directly from the root towards the highest free space under the 
given limit_pfn, for any value of limit_pfn. In fact I'd rather hope 
that the general case could get good enough to no longer need the 
max32_alloc_size bodge either (especially since pci_32bit_workaround 
will now already give up trying at all after the first failure).

Furthermore I'm also wary of growing struct iova any more than 
absolutely necessary, since people have sometimes complained about 
iova_cache usage being a large number on big, busy systems.

---

And what about all the 33 to 56-bit DMA masks? They're not all that 
uncommon and deserve some love too. FWIW I'd imagine a general algorithm 
should be something like:

- while pfn_lo > limit_pfn && left->max_gap >= size, go left
- while pfn_hi + size <= limit_pfn && right->max_gap >= size, go right
- if sufficient space to right, insert right and stop.
- if left->max_gap > size, go left, else go up and left
- repeat

(If anything that then leans towards maintaining a dummy node down at 
start_pfn, such that we could avoid a special case for allocating off 
the leftmost end of the tree...)

I guess another question to ask these days is perhaps whether rbtree is 
still even the best choice at all, or if something newer like maple tree 
might be able to be even better?

Thanks,
Robin.

2026-05-28.patch_summary: This patch series addresses performance issues caused by linear searches in alloc_iova() by converting the iova rbtree to an augmented rbtree, which maintains O(log n) complexity for all relevant operations. The change allows alloc_iova() to also be O(log n), preventing soft lockups that can occur when multiple CPUs fall into the linear search trap. The patches were written by Claude and reviewed by Rik van Riel, with additional testing and self-tests included.

reviews.author: Rik Riel (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that an anchor node is still needed in the augmented rbtree to keep track of the largest gap, opting for a simpler approach using an anchor node instead of special casing.

Author acknowledged that walking the rbtree from the root is sufficient and can find the correct address range in O(log n) time, no further revision planned.

Author acknowledged that the augmented rbtree approach might not be optimal, agreed to consider an alternative 'maple tree' data structure in a future version (v3)

Author is addressing a concern about memory usage and allocation efficiency in the iova slab cache. They acknowledge that dropping SLAB_HWCACHE_ALIGN would reduce memory use per iova, but want to test this further before posting as v3.
sentiment_signals: no need for additional augmented data
reviews.raw_body:
We kind of still need the anchor node, as the highest thing
in the rbtree, so we can use that as bookkeeping for the
largest gap.

Without it, we would not know there was an enormous 64 bit
gap if the first iova allocation is made with a 32 bit
mask.

We could add special casing, but the anchor node might be
simpler and smaller.

---

You are right, we do not need this. Walking the rbtree
from the root can bring us to the right address range
in O(log n) time, without any need for additional
augmented data.

---

I have this implemented here now.

I can send out v3 if you would like to see that version,
but you are right that the maple tree may be better!

---

Doing this allows the struct iova to shrink from
40 bytes down to 16 bytes. However the iova slab
cache seems to specify SLAB_HWCACHE_ALIGN, resulting
in 64 bytes used per iova.

If we drop SLAB_HWCACHE_ALIGN and assume 70-90%
full maple tree nodes (I assume many iova requests
are of same/similar size - let me know if that's
wrong), we are looking at a reduction of memory
use per iova from 64 bytes allocated, to around
44-60 bytes per iova on average.

This is with allocation becoming O(log n).

Maple tree may be the way to go.

Let me go test this code, and I'd be happy
to post this as v3, instead of the latest
augmented rbtree code :)


-- 
All Rights Reversed.

2026-05-29.patch_summary: This patch series addresses performance issues caused by linear searches in alloc_iova() by converting the iova rbtree to an augmented rbtree, which maintains O(log n) complexity for all relevant operations. The change allows alloc_iova() to also be O(log n), preventing soft lockups that can occur when multiple CPUs fall into the linear search trap. The patches were written by Claude and reviewed by Rik van Riel, with additional testing and self-tests included.
